content:
Jules Hedges said:

I'm getting concerned that "some people talked about it on Zulip in 2020" will be the new "some people talked about it at a conference in Buffalo in 1965"

Nathanael Arkor said:

There's a major difference, in that all the messages sent on Zulip are stored permanently, so it's possible to reference conversations here. In terms of folklore, this seems like a strict improvement.

Jules Hedges said:

I agree it's a strict improvement, but I don't think it's a very strict improvement. My hypothetical Buffalo conference was 55 years ago, wanna bet that the links into Zulip will still be alive in 2075?

Nathanael Arkor said:

It'd certainly be worth trying to make sure we have regular backups, although that's a conversation for #general: meta.
Anyone have any idea whether this is even possible?
The Lean Zulip has an archive website here: https://leanprover-community.github.io/archive/
https://zulip.readthedocs.io/en/latest/production/export-and-import.html
https://github.com/zulip/zulip-archive
latter link is apparently what lean uses

Jules Hedges said:

Anyone have any idea whether this is even possible?

Yes, we discussed this in the very beginning
@Christian Williams said he'll do periodical backups.
That was before Zulip started hosting the server, and the need for manual backups became less important, though. I'm not sure if that was still the intention now.
My thought was that research is visibly happening sometimes on here, so sooner or later somebody might want to cite it
Yeah, it's easy to back up - I just did it again. Eventually we'll put it up on the same site that we're planning to have guidelines, code of conduct etc.
The problem Jules mentions is a real one; it happened at the n-Category Café too, and that's why we (mainly Urs) invented the nLab.
He and David Corfield pressured everyone who proved or collected really interesting facts to put them on the nLab.
Other people complained about this pressure, so Urs left the n-Café in a huff and moved his base of operations to the nLab and its associated nForum.
So he's basically documenting his research on the nLab.
Some people complain about the nLab being hard to understand, but that's because it's serving a dual purpose: explaining lots of higher category theory, and also being Urs' "research notebook".
I suppose the ideal thing is to pressure everyone here to write up their interesting results on the nLab too, which might get similar complaints
I think hectoring people to put their results on the nLab (or to do anything, actually) tends to be counterproductive.
Of course I changed "pressuring" to "hectoring" just now.
But for a while anytime anyone said any really nice theorem on the nCafe, Urs or David would ask them to put it on the nLab.

They weren't really "hectoring", but the constant refrain got on people's nerves. I tried to explain that conversations on the n-Cafe were converstations, and that they were doing the equivalent of listening to a math discussion in a cafe and telling people "Hey, that's cool! Write it down in this book!"
Anyway, it caused a deeply damaging schism in the n-Category Cafe crowd.
So now we have an opportunity to do something a bit better.
When people say interesting facts here, I do wish they were stored on the nLab (or somewhere).
I sometimes do the work myself.
Interesting new research is a bit different.

Maybe the perfect solution I can imagine is a one-click button where any user on here can add any post to a mass-curated list of "good posts", which would then be tractable to sort through by hand. (That's if we had programming fairies)
As a bulletin board moderator for years I'm very pleased with the display of social intelligence here.
Programming fairy checking in. Any work?
I was just thinking out loud... in any case is it even possible to hack on Zulip-the-software while still being cloud hosted by Zulip-the-company?
Just add a nice feature to Zulip that everyone would like to have access to.
I already provide long term storage for different wisdom traditions. John Baez says wise stuff, so we're good.

As long as Zulip has a decent search function, I think it's enough. Also, you have links for single messages, so you just drop one link as a citation and whoever clicks it gets to that point in the conversation.
If I may add a suggestion, it may be worth to upload periodical backups on IPFS (a decentralized filesystem that allows to preserve data even if single computers explode).

(As an example, and for the joy of recursion, this is the link referring to the message where I say that messages can be referred to with links: https://categorytheory.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/229122-general.3A-meta/topic/backups.3F/near/195759997 )

Jules Hedges said:

Maybe the perfect solution I can imagine is a one-click button where any user on here can add any post to a mass-curated list of "good posts", which would then be tractable to sort through by hand. (That's if we had programming fairies)

I like this. One could start with a simple list of links to 'closed' conversations to sum up and write up. The list by itself would be valuable. imho.

I would imagine the hope is that, as @Matteo Capucci says, truly valuable comments and discussions could be written up somewhere more accessible, rather than relying on links to various comments. (That is, a list of "interesting snippets overheard at a conference 50 years ago", while entertaining, is less useful than a paper fleshing out the ideas in the snippets.)
This is an optimistic assumption, I admit.

Right, one hopes that any research that happens here will end up fully written up in papers (with suitable credits given) rather than mere statements being given followed by "the proof is on this invitation-only forum"
Idally, if someone ends up seriously pursuing a discussion here, the product of that pursue should become the 'write up'.
I think the main drawback with this solution is to have people be aware of its existence and, as it was pointed out above, to make people care about maintaining such an index.
OTOH, I think about 20 people here contribute in a substantial part to 80% of the conversations Pareto intensifies

Something to this effect should probably end up in the participation guidelines: that people involved in a discussion should suitably invited to participate when said discussion is to be turned into "research in a more official form". Also a standard acknowledgement line that people can put in their papers would be valuable.

If discussion material only forms a small part of a larger body of work, a short pre-print write-up of the discussion under the names of those involved is probably a good compromise to keep fair accreditation
There's a short pre-print entitled "the other pullback lemma" that came out of similar kinds of discussion on StackExchange; making that kind of write-up policy seems healthy?

I think writing papers is over-optimistic. If for example it turns out (in the thread I was originally quoting from) that Petri nets form a topos and the proof is a few lines long, nobody will write a paper just with that fact because it's far too small, it'll become folklore
In that case, it should go on the nLab with the few lines of proof. Then it is no longer folklore.
Yeah, in fact the nLab is basically folklore distilled, afaik
Since things becoming folklore is explicitly what we want to avoid, we should at least make sure that someone writes it down somewhere else which is more accessible.

If the category of Petri nets (as I define it) turned out to be a topos, I'd run around the neighborhood telling everyone the shocking news. Then I'd think of ways to use it in a paper.

I think it's mainly less shocking results that might get lost.

@Morgan Rogers I think indexing, summing up and distilling an nlab page are progressive improvements toward the goal of defolklorization of chats
What I believe is important is to have low effort, still-somewhat-useful actionable so that we could get people involved. Otherwise we'll just be too lazy to do it.
So a rule of thumb could be "if you ever find yourself looking something up on Zulip, record it in nLab before using it elsewhere"?

Incidentally, @Mario Román's preprint https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.04526 already references something that happened here, but indirectly with a footnote and without a direct link to the post: "This particular shape comes from a question by Nathaniel Virgo oncategorytheory.zulipchat.com."
if someone references something i say in one of their papers, can i put it on my cv

Jules Hedges said:

I think writing papers is over-optimistic. If for example it turns out (in the thread I was originally quoting from) that Petri nets form a topos and the proof is a few lines long, nobody will write a paper just with that fact because it's far too small, it'll become folklore

Martti Karvonen and I are writing a paper based on a discussion that started here, FWIW.
